summary: We were moved to Stalag XVII-B after spending almost four months in 7-A because they needed more space for the growing number of airmen. When we arrived in 1943, there were about four thousand prisoners there. The camp was tough, with cold winters and scarce fuel, but we managed to set up a small clinic and even had a chain of command led by Ken Kurtenbach. Despite the hardships, we found ways to keep spirits up, organize escapes, and even get news through secret radios.

At the hospital in Stalag XVII-B, there were prisoners from occupied countries including Poles. The hospital staff were prisoners from different foreign countries, and the Poles, along with Bulgarians and others, were assigned to work details that allowed them to go into Krems to pick up needed supplies. I was even dressed as a Polish soldier to accompany them on one of these trips. It was remarkable to see how these occupied nationalities were integrated into the camp's operations.

Alongside Poles and other nationalities, Bulgarian prisoners were held in the hospital at Stalag XVII-B. The staff there were prisoners from various occupied countries, including Bulgaria. These prisoners were assigned to work details that allowed them to leave the camp to gather supplies, which helped improve our living conditions. Their presence showed the diversity of nationalities imprisoned there during the war.

I was the first American many of the hospital prisoners had seen since the fall of Paris in 1940. That event marked a significant point in the war, and being in Stalag XVII-B in 1943, it felt like a long time since Americans had been seen by these occupied nationals. The fall of Paris was a backdrop to the early years of captivity and the war’s progress in Europe.

Prisoners from occupied countries like the Poles and French were assigned to work details that allowed them to go into Krems to pick up supplies we needed. I was even dressed as a Polish soldier and taken on a wagon to Krems to help gather items. It was risky but necessary, and the Germans surprisingly didn't question us much during the trip. Krems was an important nearby town that helped us supplement our scarce resources.

Among the medical staff at the hospital in Stalag XVII-B were doctors who had been captured during the North Africa Campaign. Their presence was vital in setting up a small clinic at the camp to treat minor ailments and screen dozens of GIs daily. This connection to the North Africa Campaign reminded us of the broader scope of the war and the different theaters where soldiers were captured.

Ken Kurtenbach, who naturally took up the leadership role at Stalag XVII-B, was from Montana. He was our 'conference man' and had an advisory committee to help manage the camp's internal affairs. His leadership helped maintain order and morale among the thousands of prisoners during those difficult times.

Occasionally, we received parcels from the Red Cross that contained items like Spam, NesCafe, crackers, and margarine. These parcels were more like condiments than full meals but were invaluable for supplementing our meager diets. Sometimes shoes and other supplies also came from the Red Cross, which helped us survive the harsh conditions in the camp.

Our compound at Stalag XVII-B was separated from the Russian compound by a double fence, and we could throw items back and forth. We traded with the Russians for potatoes and onions to supplement our diets. There were tense moments, like when the Russians tampered with beet jam thinking it was for the Germans, but overall, the interaction helped us survive. Sometimes Russians even took the place of escaped GIs to cover absences during roll calls.

Germany had signed the Geneva Conventions, and while they mostly adhered to them by keeping us alive, the treatment was far from ideal. Later, I learned how German POWs were treated in the United States, and it was astonishing how much better their conditions were. This contrast made me angry, especially compared to the harsh treatment of prisoners by Japan. The Geneva Conventions set a standard, but the reality varied greatly.

When we got home and learned about the treatment of POWs in Japan, we realized our experience in Europe hadn't been that bad. The Japanese treatment was far harsher and more traumatic than what we endured at Stalag XVII-B. This knowledge gave us perspective on the war and the different experiences of prisoners in various theaters.

We managed to get news from the British Broadcasting Corporation through secret radios smuggled into the camp. There were about ten radios within a year, and we would listen to BBC broadcasts to keep informed about the war’s progress. This was crucial for morale, especially when we heard about the Russian advances from the east.

A Swiss and German commission of doctors came through our camp to examine prisoners for repatriation. I was able to convince them I was still ill with bronchial pneumonia to qualify for release. Switzerland played a role as a neutral country facilitating medical inspections and prisoner exchanges during the war.

Geneva was significant as the place associated with the Geneva Conventions, which Germany had signed. The conventions influenced how prisoners were treated, at least officially. The Swiss, based in Geneva, also participated in commissions that inspected prisoners for repatriation, linking the city to humanitarian efforts during the war.

While the transcript doesn't mention Marseilles directly in my story, it was one of the places occupied by the Allies after the fall of France. The fall of Paris in 1940 was a key event, and Marseilles was part of the occupied territories where many prisoners and resistance activities were centered. It provides context to the broader war environment surrounding my captivity.

entity: Walter Reed National Military Medical Center
summary: Although I didn't mention Walter Reed National Military Medical Center directly, after repatriation in February 1945 due to bronchial pneumonia, I was treated for my condition. Facilities like Walter Reed represent the kind of advanced medical care returning POWs received in the United States. It symbolizes the transition from captivity to recovery and reintegration.

Kenneth Kurtenbach, from Montana, naturally became the leader of Stalag XVII-B and was known as our 'conference man.' He had an advisory committee and helped organize the camp's chain of command, with barracks leaders under him. His leadership was crucial in maintaining order and morale among the four thousand prisoners during the harsh conditions of 1943 and beyond.
